schools.summary: Paul says 'it is not expedient (profitable) for me to glory' — boasting does him no good and is distasteful — yet, compelled by his opponents, he will go on to 'visions and revelations of the Lord'; turning from boasting of his sufferings (ch. 11) to the extraordinary visions granted him by the Lord, the highest of his credentials (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: Paul enters on this subject unwillingly, only because forced; he would rather be silent about such glories, and speaks as one constrained.

themes: It is not expedient for me doubtless to glory
I will come to visions and revelations of the Lord
Paul's reluctant turn to visions and revelations
constrained to boast

schools.summary: Paul speaks 'of a man in Christ' (a Christian) in the third person, but means himself — out of modesty and to avoid the appearance of boasting, he veils his own identity (confirmed by v.7); he confesses he does not know 'whether in the body or out of the body' he was caught up, in a state of trance, leaving the manner to God, content with the fact; 'the third heaven' is the highest heaven, the dwelling-place of God (above the aerial and the starry heavens), not the seven heavens of Jewish fable but the topmost (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: Paul does not know whether soul and body together or the soul only was taken up — the greatness of the revelation being such that he did not understand its manner; we should seek the knowledge only of what is necessary, and leave the rest to God (Deuteronomy 29:29).
Cyril: Paul, who was caught up to the third heaven and into Paradise and heard unspeakable words, is the great witness — the highest heaven being the abode of God and the blessed.

themes: I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago
caught up to the third heaven
the man caught up — Paul himself
whether in or out of the body

schools.summary: Paul repeats 'I knew such a man' and again the disclaimer 'whether in the body or out of the body, I cannot tell' — a solemn repetition that emphasizes both the reality of the event and the mystery of its manner; he refers the unknown manner wholly to God ('God knoweth'), content to leave with God what he himself cannot explain — the fact is sure, the mode beyond him; he makes no pretence of understanding the rapture (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: And I knew such a man
whether in the body, or out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth
the solemn repetition of the rapture
affirming the fact, leaving the manner to God

schools.summary: Barnes and Robertson take 'paradise' as the same place as the third heaven (v.2) — the abode of God and the blessed, named differently (Paul uses paradise as the equivalent of the third heaven). Paul 'heard unspeakable (ineffable) words which it is not lawful (or possible) for a man to utter' — either things human language cannot express, or things he was not permitted to reveal; he keeps a reverent silence (the contrast with Mohammed's puerile boasts marking true inspiration).

Calvin: these revelations were given for Paul's own strengthening, to sustain him in his arduous trials, not for publication; his silence rebukes vain curiosity about heavenly secrets (against the speculations of pseudo-Dionysius); we are to keep within bounds and seek only what the Lord has revealed.

Gill (with the ancients — Clement of Alexandria, Theophylact, Oecumenius, and Ellicott): paradise is a distinct stage or place — a second rapture, from the third heaven on into paradise, the place of the righteous souls' rest (the 'up to' the one and 'into' the other suggesting two raptures).

debates.question: Is 'paradise' the same place as the third heaven, or a distinct second rapture?

sides.position: The same place, named differently — paradise is the third heaven
A distinct stage/place — a second rapture into the rest of the righteous souls

themes: caught up into paradise
heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter
the rapture into paradise and the unutterable words
one rapture or two

schools.summary: Paul's careful turn: 'of such an one (the man caught up, himself viewed as the recipient of revelation) will I glory' — he may boast of that — 'yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities'; of himself personally, Paul will boast only of his 'infirmities (weaknesses, sufferings),' not of his revelations as if they were his own achievement; he glories in what shows his weakness, not his strength (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul keeps the glory of the revelation distinct from himself, lest he seem to claim merit; his only personal boast is in the weaknesses that magnify God's grace.

themes: Of such an one will I glory: yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities
glorying in the favoured man yet only in his infirmities
boasting in weakness, not achievement
humility maintained

schools.summary: Paul says that even 'if I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool' — his boast would be no empty folly, 'for I will say the truth'; his claims are all true and could be justly made; yet he 'forbears (refrains)' — holds back from boasting — 'lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or heareth of me'; he will not be rated higher than his conduct and teaching warrant (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul would have men judge him by what is plainly seen in his life and ministry, not by extraordinary revelations; he refuses to trade on his visions for reputation.

themes: though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool; for I will say the truth
but now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be
Paul forbearing to boast lest he be overrated
judged by his life, not his visions

schools.summary: To keep Paul from spiritual pride after such revelations, 'a thorn (skolops — a stake, splinter, or sharp thorn) in the flesh' was 'given' him (the word implying a gift, a favour), 'the messenger of Satan to buffet me'; most naturally some chronic bodily ailment (eye-disease/ophthalmia, neuralgia or headache, epilepsy, malaria, or the like) — a physical malady that persisted; Barnes judges all confident identification of it mere conjecture, and counts it a blessing that we do not know, since each has his own thorn (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: 'those act a ridiculous part who think Paul was tempted to lust — we must repudiate that fancy'; rather the thorn comprehends every kind of temptation with which Paul was exercised against the not-yet-regenerate part of the soul ('the flesh'); called the messenger of Satan because all temptations are his, yet God made medicinal what was in its own nature deadly, subduing Paul's pride (the last and most obstinate vice) by this rod.

Clarke (with Chrysostom): the thorn is the persecution and distress from his opponents — especially the false apostle at Corinth and adversaries like Hymeneus and Alexander — whose tearing of the church was as painful to Paul as a stake in the flesh; not a bodily infirmity. The sensual-lust view of the Latin and Romish interpreters (which Ellicott discusses and partly entertains as a supplement) is set aside.

debates.question: What was Paul's 'thorn in the flesh'?

sides.position: A chronic bodily ailment (eye-disease, headache/neuralgia, epilepsy) — though Paul's reticence leaves it uncertain
Persecution and distress from his opponents, especially the false apostle
Every kind of temptation against the flesh (but NOT sensual lust, which is 'ridiculous')
Sensual/carnal temptation (the Latin and Romish Fathers) — rejected as ridiculous by Calvin and Barnes

themes: there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me
lest I should be exalted above measure
the thorn given to keep Paul humble
the crux of the thorn's nature

schools.summary: Paul 'besought the Lord thrice' — prayed earnestly and repeatedly (as Christ prayed thrice in Gethsemane) — 'that it (the thorn) might depart from me'; he sought relief from the painful affliction; 'the Lord' here is Christ (as v.9 shows, 'the power of Christ'), Paul praying directly to Christ, an incidental but real testimony to Christ's deity and his right to be invoked (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): troubles drive us to prayer, and if relief is not granted at once we are to persevere; Paul's thrice-repeated prayer teaches importunity, and that he prayed to Christ shows Christ's deity.

themes: For this thing I besought the Lord thrice, that it might depart from me
Paul's threefold prayer for the thorn's removal
praying directly to Christ
importunity in prayer

schools.summary: Christ answered Paul's prayer not by removing the thorn but with a better gift: 'my grace is sufficient for thee' — the support of his grace would be enough to bear it; and 'my strength is made perfect (displayed in its fulness) in weakness' — Christ's power shines most clearly when human strength fails; so Paul will 'most gladly' glory in his weaknesses, that 'the power of Christ may rest (episkenoo, tabernacle, dwell) upon him,' welcoming weakness as the dwelling-place of Christ's power (Barnes, Robertson).

Wesley (with Henry and Calvin): when God does not take away the trouble but gives grace enough, we have no reason to complain; his strength is made perfect in our weakness — when we are weak in ourselves, then we are strong in the grace of Christ.

themes: My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness
that the power of Christ may rest upon me
sufficient grace and strength perfected in weakness
weakness the dwelling-place of Christ's power

schools.summary: Paul, having learned the lesson, now 'takes pleasure (is content, even glad)' in a whole train of trials — 'infirmities, reproaches, necessities, persecutions, distresses' — endured 'for Christ's sake'; the summing truth being 'when I am weak (in myself), then am I strong (in Christ's power),' for his very weakness is the occasion of Christ's strength resting on him; conscious weakness is the path to divine strength (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry, Wesley): this is the Christian paradox — the believer is never so strong as when, emptied of self-confidence, he leans wholly on Christ; Paul rejoices in what drives him to that dependence.

themes: I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses for Christ's sake
for when I am weak, then am I strong
pleasure in weakness, for then he is strong
strength in conscious weakness

schools.summary: Paul says his boasting has made him 'a fool,' but 'ye have compelled me' — the Corinthians, by listening to his detractors, forced him to it (the folly laid at their door); they themselves 'ought to have commended' him (defended his character) instead of forcing him to commend himself; and he is 'in nothing behind the very chiefest (the most eminent, super-extra) apostles' — equal to the greatest — yet 'though I be nothing' in himself (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: the balance of dignity and humility — Paul is behind the chiefest apostles in nothing as to office and labour, yet nothing in himself; high in calling, lowly in self-estimate.

themes: I am become a fool in glorying; ye have compelled me
in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles, though I be nothing
forced into foolish boasting
equal to the chiefest apostles yet nothing

schools.summary: Paul appeals to 'the signs of an apostle' that 'were wrought among you' — the marks that authenticated a true apostle, performed in their very sight; the threefold description 'signs (significant tokens), and wonders (marvels exciting awe), and mighty deeds (works of power, miracles)' — the supernatural credentials of his office; and these 'in all patience (endurance),' amid much suffering and opposition, his patient endurance itself a mark of his apostleship (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the miracles and patient endurance together authenticated Paul's apostleship among the Corinthians, who had themselves witnessed the proof.

themes: the signs of an apostle were wrought among you in all patience
in signs, and wonders, and mighty deeds
the signs of an apostle wrought among the Corinthians
the credentials of the true apostle

schools.summary: Paul asks 'what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches' — the Corinthians lacked nothing other churches had — 'except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you' (did not take support from them); the biting irony being that the only 'wrong' he did them was refusing to be a financial burden, supporting himself rather than take their money, so he asks ironically, 'forgive me this wrong' (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul's enemies had twisted his refusal of support into a grievance (as if it proved he was no true apostle, or did not love them); he answers with pointed irony, exposing the absurdity.

themes: what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches, except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you
forgive me this wrong
Paul's irony that his only wrong was not burdening them
self-support twisted into a grievance

schools.summary: Paul announces his readiness to come 'the third time' and repeats that he 'will not be burdensome' — he will again refuse their support, keeping his settled practice; his motive being a beautiful disavowal of self-interest: 'I seek not yours (your money, your goods) but you (yourselves, your souls, your salvation)'; and as 'parents lay up for the children,' not children for parents, so Paul, their spiritual father, will provide for them rather than draw from them — his love paternal (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul's love is that of a parent for children; he desires their souls, not their substance, and like a father lays up for them rather than burdening them.

themes: the third time I am ready to come to you; and I will not be burdensome to you
I seek not yours, but you... the parents for the children
seeking the Corinthians themselves, not their goods
paternal love

schools.summary: Paul declares he 'will very gladly spend (lay out all he has) and be spent (be utterly used up, exhausted, even unto death) for you' — he will give not only his goods but himself for their good; the grief beneath being that 'the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved' — the more Paul loves them, the less he is loved in return, they requiting his abundant love with coldness; yet this does not lessen his devotion (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: true pastoral love loves on, even when unrequited; Paul's willingness to be spent for those who love him less is the mark of a genuine shepherd, imitating Christ.

themes: I will very gladly spend and be spent for you
though the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved
gladness to spend and be spent, though loved the less
love unrequited yet undiminished

schools.summary: Paul ironically states the slander of his opponents: granting that he 'did not burden' them directly, they charged that 'being crafty, I caught you with guile' — that he took their money indirectly, by cunning, through his agents (insinuating he secretly enriched himself through the collection or the brethren he sent); a slander on his integrity which he now refutes (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: that even Paul's refusal of support was twisted into a proof of cunning shows the malice of his foes; he repeats their charge in order to expose and demolish it.

themes: I did not burden you: nevertheless, being crafty, I caught you with guile
Paul taking up the slander that he caught them by craft
the insinuation of secret gain
a slander he will refute

schools.summary: Paul answers the slander with a direct challenge: 'Did I make a gain of you (defraud you, profit at your expense) by any of them whom I sent unto you?' — let them name a single instance; the appeal to fact, for Paul sent various messengers (Titus and others) and not one had ever taken advantage of the Corinthians; the charge of indirect gain collapses against the evidence, and Paul appeals confidently to their own knowledge (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: Did I make a gain of you by any of them whom I sent unto you
Paul's challenge that he gained nothing through his messengers
the appeal to fact
his agents' integrity

schools.summary: Paul names a specific case: 'I desired (urged) Titus, and with him I sent a brother' — and asks, 'Did Titus make a gain of you?' The answer is plainly no; Titus had taken nothing. And he and Titus 'walked in the same spirit (disposition) and the same steps (conduct)' — there was no difference between them, both acting with the same disinterested integrity; since Titus, Paul's representative, took nothing and acted exactly as Paul did, the charge that Paul cunningly profited through his agents is shown to be baseless (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

themes: Did Titus make a gain of you? walked we not in the same spirit? walked we not in the same steps
the appeal to Titus's blameless conduct
the same spirit and the same steps
the charge shown baseless

schools.summary: Paul asks 'think ye that we excuse (defend) ourselves unto you?' — as if his whole concern were merely to clear his own name before them; he disclaims any such low motive, and speaks 'before God in Christ' — in God's presence, with full sincerity, not for human approval but as accountable to God; his true purpose being that he does 'all things, dearly beloved, for your edifying (building up),' for the Corinthians' spiritual good, not his own vindication (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): Paul's defense is no mere self-justification before men, but is spoken sincerely before God and aimed wholly at the edification of those he loves.

themes: think ye that we excuse ourselves unto you? we speak before God in Christ
we do all things, dearly beloved, for your edifying
speaking before God, all for their edifying
the defense aimed at building up

schools.summary: Paul 'fears, lest when I come, I shall not find you such as I would' (not in the state he desires) 'and that I shall be found unto you such as ye would not' — that he may have to come in severity, which they would not wish; the list of evils he fears still persist among them: 'debates (strife), envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings (secret slanders), swellings (pride, conceit), tumults (disorders)' — the sins of a divided, contentious church (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul names these sins not to insult them but in loving fear, that they may amend before he comes and spare him the painful necessity of discipline.

themes: I fear, lest, when I come, I shall not find you such as I would
lest there be debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, tumults
Paul's fear of finding sins and disorders
loving apprehension that they amend

schools.summary: Paul fears that, on his return, 'my God will humble me among you' — that he will be brought low, grieved and ashamed, by finding sin still among them (their sin his humiliation) — and that he must 'bewail (mourn over) many which have sinned already, and have not repented' of 'the uncleanness and fornication and lasciviousness which they have committed'; those who had fallen into gross sins of impurity and remained impenitent, their hardness causing him deep sorrow (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).
Calvin: Paul grieves, not in anger but in pastoral sorrow, over the impenitent; their failure to repent of gross sin is his humiliation, and he warns them that they may turn before he must act.

themes: lest... my God will humble me among you
I shall bewail many which have sinned already, and have not repented of the uncleanness and fornication and lasciviousness
Paul's fear of mourning the unrepentant impure
pastoral grief over impenitent sin
